techniques.evidence_quote: using one access token and three service account credentials that had been taken, and that we failed to rotate, after the Okta compromise of October 2023
we were (for the second time) the victim of a compromise of Okta's systems which resulted in a threat actor gaining access to a set of credentials
the threat actor started probing and performing reconnaissance of our systems beginning on November 14, looking for a way to use the credentials and what systems were accessible
They attempted to log into our Okta instance and were denied access. They attempted access to the Cloudflare Dashboard and were denied access.
The threat actor searched the wiki for things like remote access, secret, client-secret, openconnect, cloudflared, and token

techniques.technique_name: Data from Information Repositories: Code Repositories
techniques.evidence_quote: the threat actor viewed 120 code repositories (out of a total of 11,904 repositories)
They accessed 36 Jira tickets (out of a total of 2,059,357 tickets) and 202 wiki pages
The threat actor used the Smartsheet credential to create an Atlassian account that looked like a normal Cloudflare user
They added this user to a number of groups within Atlassian so that they'd have persistent access
Sliver was installed using the ScriptRunner for Jira plugin
the threat actor was able to install the Sliver Adversary Emulation Framework, which is a widely used tool and framework that red teams and attackers use to enable 'C2' (command and control)
they used this to attempt lateral movement... attempted to gain access to a non-production console server in our São Paulo, Brazil data center
The threat actor adds the Smartsheet service account to an administrator group.
the threat actor used the Atlassian Bitbucket git archive feature on 76 repositories to download them to the Atlassian server
